title: Community Protection and Wildfire Resilience Act
short_title: Community Protection and Wildfire Resilience Act
plain_summary: This bill creates a new grant program to help communities protect themselves from wildfires. Communities can get up to ten million dollars for wildfire protection projects or up to two hundred fifty thousand dollars to make protection plans. The bill also expands an existing grant program so it can pay for making buildings more fire-resistant, like upgrading roofs or siding. Additionally, the bill requires studies on wildfire protection programs and making sure emergency workers have good radio communication during fires.
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on Science, Space, and Technology, and in addition to the Committees on Natural Resources, and Agriculture, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each

sections.title: Section 1  Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Community Protection and Wildfire Resilience Act.

sections.title: Section 2  Definitions
sections.text: In this law, these words have these meanings. Administrator means the person who runs the Federal Emergency Management Agency. Chief means the person who runs the Forest Service. Community protection and wildfire resilience plan means a plan that is created by an eligible group working with local communities, governments, Indian tribes, police, firefighters, first responders, fire managers, utility companies, nonprofit groups doing related work locally, and state agencies that handle fire prevention, emergency response, public safety, environmental protection, and forest management. The plan must include strategies and activities for improving early warning systems, public education, alert systems, evacuation planning and execution, and access for first responders. It must address vulnerable people like elderly people, children, people with disabilities, and homeless people. It must include ways to strengthen critical infrastructure and homes through incentive programs. It must apply community-wide defensible space projects developed with the groups mentioned above across connected areas. It must build local ability to carry out and oversee the plan. It must include strategic land use planning. It must educate community members about improving community resilience. It must coordinate any existing wildfire plans like community wildfire protection plans or community emergency evacuation plans. It must include information from a map created under a two thousand eighteen law. The plan can be made up of existing plans or efforts as long as they meet these requirements. Critical infrastructure means any public safety, health, education, transportation, communications, water, or power utility infrastructure or any private infrastructure needed to preserve community safety, resilience, or continued operation when facing a wildfire threat. Defensible space project means a project conducted within one hundred feet around a home, business, or administrative building that involves vegetation pruning like yearly removal of tree seedlings and saplings, lower limbs of mature trees, cutting grasses and reducing density and continuity of shrubs, and removal of most small twigs and leaves. If the Administrator chooses, and if a state has established by law a more restrictive definition, it can use the state law definition instead. Eligible entity means a state, an Indian tribe, a unit of general local or regional government including a fire protection district or municipal fire department, a volunteer fire department, or a collaborative effort of at least two of these entities. Program means the grant program established under section three. State means a state of the United States, the District of Columbia, Guam, Puerto Rico, the Virgin Islands, American Samoa, the Northern Mariana Islands, and any other territory or possession of the United States.

sections.title: Section 3  Community Protection and Wildfire Resilience Grant Program
sections.text: The Administrator, working through the United States Fire Administrator and with the Chief, must create a program within one year of this law being enacted. This program will be separate from another existing disaster relief program. Under this program, the Administrator will give grants to eligible entities. If an eligible entity already has a community protection and wildfire resilience plan, they can use the grant to carry out projects described in their plan. If an eligible entity does not have such a plan, they can use the grant to develop one. The Administrator, working through the United States Fire Administrator and with the Chief, must establish criteria for awarding grants within one year of this law being enacted. Money from the program can only be used for projects and activities in communities that existed when this law was enacted. When awarding grants, the Administrator must give priority to eligible entities that will carry out projects or plans supporting a community located in an area with high risk for fire or wildfire, as shown by a state wildfire hazard map or a map created under a two thousand eighteen law. An eligible entity that receives a grant must use the money to carry out projects that support a diverse portfolio of community protection and wildfire resilience strategies described in their plan. These grants can be for up to ten million dollars. An eligible entity that receives a grant may use the money to develop a community protection and wildfire resilience plan. These grants can be for up to two hundred fifty thousand dollars. When carrying out a project using grant money, the grant recipient must, as much as possible, give preference to hiring contractors and workers from the area where the project is being done, including partnering with local corps groups like AmeriCorps or a conservation corps. The non-federal share of the cost of carrying out a project using grant funds must be at least twenty five percent of the cost for projects carried out under the main grant program and zero percent for plan development grants. This non-federal share can be provided by a state, local government, Indian tribe, nonprofit organization, private industry, or a combination of these, or through volunteer hours and donated goods and services. For projects that serve low-income communities, this share can be in the form of a low-interest federal loan through the Community Disaster Loan program. The Administrator may waive or reduce the non-federal share amount when carrying out the program. One billion dollars is authorized to be appropriated to carry out the program for each of fiscal years two thousand twenty five through two thousand twenty nine.

sections.title: Section 4  Government Accountability Office Report
sections.text: Within one year of this law being enacted, the Comptroller General of the United States must publish a report on authorities and programs of the federal government that are available to protect communities from wildfires. The report must include an assessment of obstacles to implementing such programs, including gaps in funding.

sections.title: Section 5  Government Accountability Office Study
sections.text: Within one year of this law being enacted, the Comptroller General of the United States must publish a study. The study must include an assessment of the potential for a community protection and wildfire resilience plan to qualify for a certification that identifies a level of wildfire survivability and resilience, and methods that could be used by the federal government to encourage insurance companies to accept such a certification. The study must also identify any metrics that could be provided to insurance companies as assurance that a community has wildfire resilience measures in place.

sections.title: Section 6  Updating List of At-Risk Communities
sections.text: This section changes the definition of at-risk communities in an existing two thousand three law. The new definition describes these communities as groups of homes and other structures with basic infrastructure and services like utilities and collectively maintained transportation routes within or adjacent to federal land. Within one hundred eighty days of this law being enacted, and every five years after that, the Administrator, working through the United States Fire Administrator and with the Chief, must develop and publish a map showing at-risk communities as defined in the changed law, including tribal at-risk communities.

sections.title: Section 7  Report on Radio Communications
sections.text: Within two years of this law being enacted, the Administrator, working through the United States Fire Administrator and with the Chief, must prepare a report about insufficient radio frequencies, barriers to radio frequency interoperability (ability to work together), and available products and technologies for overcoming barriers to interoperability for wildfire management. When preparing the report, the Administrator must cooperate with the Secretary of Agriculture, agencies responsible for managing federal land, state fire marshals, state and local emergency response agencies, tribal fire departments and emergency managers, and municipal fire departments, fire protection districts, and volunteer fire departments in relevant communities. The report must include a determination of whether these entities can communicate by radio during a potential fire suppression effort for a community conflagration (large destructive fire). It must determine whether the reserved radio frequencies are sufficient for wildfire management or whether additional frequencies are recommended to be reserved or obtained, listed by type and location. It must analyze commercially available technology and products to enable radios from multiple agencies operating on different radio frequencies to work together. If the Administrator determines that any entities do not have the ability to communicate, the report must include a plan for ensuring such entities would be able to communicate adequately during a fire suppression effort for a community conflagration.

sections.title: Section 8  Amendment to Community Wildfire Defense Grant Program to Allow Structure Hardening
sections.text: This section changes an existing law called the Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act to allow structure hardening projects. The changes add covered projects to the allowed uses of grants. Covered projects are defined as the construction, modification, or maintenance of a structure to make the structure resistant to the intrusion of flames or embers. They also include the modification of a structure or an area adjacent to a structure, including vegetation and miscellaneous structures like garages, sheds, and fencing, if done to reduce the exposure of the structure to direct contact from flame, radiation, or embers from wildfire, and with consideration for the potential effects on the structure of nearby combustible features including vegetation, vehicles, and miscellaneous structures like garages, sheds, and fencing.
